artifacts.content:
Event: Dr. Chang – talk about sleep + panic before crits
Date: Thursday, 2021-09-09
Time: 3:30–4:20 PM
Location: Illinois State University Student Counseling Services, DeGarmo Hall – 3rd floor

Notes:
- This is the every-other-Thursday session with Dr. Melissa Chang. Keep this block sacred; no AIAS meetings, no fab lab shifts. Last spring I kept blowing off anything that wasn’t studio and ended up a mess by midterms.
- Main things to bring up:
  • The way my chest tightens the week before reviews, especially night before desk crits. That one crit in Building Systems where I thought I’d pass out is still stuck in my head.
  • Sleep: still doing the 2 a.m. doom scroll after studio instead of actually resting. Need strategies that are realistic during comps semester.
  • The March 2021 ER trip to Advocate Bromenn for the stomach pain. They called it stress-related gastritis and put me on omeprazole. I keep the bottle in my studio locker now so I don’t forget, but I’m still nervous it’ll flare if I pull another all-nighter.
- Ask her how to set boundaries so I don’t stack late nights back-to-back. She’ll probably remind me again that ending up at Bromenn at 3 a.m. over “stupid stomach” pain is not worth one more section drawing.
- Also want to talk about feeling like I’m “translating” between worlds all the time—family, studio, AIAS. Feels like I’m never fully off.

Reminder: walk over from Willow St. by 3:05, bring water, phone on Do Not Disturb. Text Caro I’ll be back by 4:45 so she doesn’t worry if I go straight to studio after.

metadata.title: Dr. Chang – talk about sleep + panic before crits
metadata.location: ISU Student Counseling Services, DeGarmo Hall

artifacts.content:
From: jordan.e.ramirez@gmail.com
To: sofia.ramirez17@gmail.com
Date: 2021-10-03
Subject: hey kiddo + essay thoughts

Hey Sof,

Just finished my Sunday desk clean-up and our speakerphone call with Mom and Dad, so I wanted to send you a longer note before the week gets wild again.

First: I read your draft about wanting to do civil engineering. It’s really good. I like the part where you talk about buses never lining up with practice and how sidewalks just stop in your neighborhood. That’s the kind of detail admissions people actually remember.

I keep thinking about that email from your assistant principal back on May 6 — “Incident involving Daniel Ramirez – May 6.” I still have it starred. The way that kid called our block “ghetto” and then Daniel got suspended after the fight… it’s exactly why I care so much about how schools and public spaces feel. If you want, you could mention that moment in the essay, not in a dramatic way, just as something that made you start noticing which neighborhoods get investment and which don’t.

Things here are busy. Studio is all adaptive reuse this term (vacant warehouse into housing + community space), which makes me think a lot about Aurora. Dad said on the phone he’s been driving past more empty buildings on his delivery routes again. I hate that he’s still picking up those sketchy late-night courier shifts in Chicago to chip away at Mom’s Rush Copley bills from the 2019 surgery. Mom sounded tired tonight when she mentioned “Miguel’s side job,” but she also said the debt is finally moving in the right direction.

I’m okay, just tired. I’m trying not to repeat last spring, when I ended up at Advocate Bromenn in Normal with that stress stomach thing. So: more sleep, less caffeine (in theory), and walks around campus with podcasts about housing nerd stuff.

Send me your next draft when you’re ready. Proud of you.

Love,
Jordan

Thread: Jordan 🐝 / Mariah 💻
Date: 2022-02-18

Mariah: you alive?? 👀

Jordan: lol barely. just got out of AIAS board meeting. why did we think a multi-school firm crawl was a chill idea??

Mariah: because we’re masochists who love spreadsheets

Jordan: accurate. also I just paid the Discover card again and I’m mad at past us for going to that national AIAS thing last spring 😂

Mariah: how bad is it?

Jordan: balance is still like $450ish from the Spring 2022 trip. I opened that stupid Discover student card at the ISU Credit Union in January and now it’s 21.99% APR and I’m only managing the $35 minimum.

Mariah: ughhh. once your Lopez + Kwan hours go up this summer you’ll kill it off.

Jordan: that’s the plan. just can’t put any more flights on it. so if there’s no travel stipend for this next conference, I might have to skip.

Mariah: understandable. also, related to conferences / leadership stuff… are you still thinking about that session on “being out at work”? you mentioned it last week.

Jordan: yeah. I’m lowkey hoping someone talks about being out OR not. we’ve been together since October 2020 and most of my family still thinks “Mariah from studio” is just my crit partner 🙃

Mariah: hey, your pace is your pace. I don’t need to be in the group chat with your tías yet lol

Jordan: haha you definitely don’t want my conservative aunt scrolling through my photos and finding our weekend pics. that’s why the Chicago trip photos live in the “studio inspo” album.

Mariah: sneaky architect.

Jordan: anyway, I’m heading to the fab lab. text later? movie night after I finish laser cutting?

Mariah: deal. I’ll bring popcorn.

From: jordan.e.ramirez@gmail.com
To: prof.martin@ilstu.edu
Date: 2022-03-21
Subject: quick check-in + something I’ve been wrestling with

Hi Professor Martin,

Hope your break was at least a little restful. I’m back in Normal after a few days in Aurora and wanted to follow up on our hallway conversation about “gray areas” in practice.

I’ve been really excited about starting AXP through my internship at Lopez + Kwan in Chicago. The work on the Pilsen retrofit and the Little Village community meetings lines up almost perfectly with what I want to do long-term. But something happened last summer that’s been sitting wrong with me.

On June 14, 2022, my project lead there, Eric Nolan (a senior associate), messaged me in a Teams chat called “Pilsen retrofit hours.” He asked me to log 4–5 extra AXP-eligible hours for a Little Village community meeting I didn’t actually attend, “so we can show more engagement.” It was framed like rounding up, and I went along with it once, but I’ve felt uneasy ever since, especially because community engagement is the whole point of the work.

Part of why this hits a nerve is that I already had a scare with academic integrity. In Fall 2020, in Dr. Benson’s Building Systems I class, my HVAC load calc assignment got flagged for “excessive similarity” to a classmate’s. It ended with a written warning and a mandatory workshop, and it’s still in my internal department file. Since then I’ve been almost overcorrecting about credit and honesty.

I’m not asking you to fix anything, I just don’t want to shove this under the rug. If you have time for coffee sometime next week, I’d appreciate your perspective on how to navigate this without burning bridges or compromising my values.

Best,
Jordan

metadata.subject: quick check-in + something I’ve been wrestling with

artifacts.content:
Title: Fall 2022 reality check – time/energy/money
Date: 2022-08-10

Okay, honest inventory before semester starts:

TIME
- Class + comps studio schedule is basically M/W 1–5, T/Th theory + urban seminar, Friday mornings for desk crits.
- Fab lab job has to stay at ~8–10 hours/week. I can’t really cut back because I’ve been sending $150–$200 a month via Zelle to Mom since August 2020 to help with Nicor Gas and ComEd at the Aurora duplex. That winter 2019–2020 shutoff notice scared everyone, and I don’t want them that close to losing heat again.
- Thursdays after 3:30 are blocked for Dr. Chang at Counseling Services in DeGarmo Hall. Anxiety got really bad last semester around studio reviews, and those every-other-Thursday sessions (“Dr. Chang – talk about sleep + panic before crits”) are non-negotiable.

HEALTH
- Remember March 2021: ER at Advocate Bromenn in Normal, stress-induced gastritis. Omeprazole lives in the studio locker now. No back-to-back all-nighters. Protect Friday mornings for rest or quiet model building.

MONEY
- Still carrying the Discover student card balance (~$450) from the Spring 2022 AIAS national trip I put on it. 21.99% APR is brutal, so no new travel unless there’s a stipend.
- Internship hours at Lopez + Kwan: aim for 10–12 hours/week during semester, more over breaks to attack the card and keep sending money home.

BOUNDARIES
- Say no to extra AIAS events if they fall during counseling or family call time.
- One full off-screen block each weekend (walk around campus with a housing podcast, no laptop).

Goal: finish this year proud, not wrecked.

From: jordan.e.ramirez@gmail.com
To: caro.alvarez@ilstu.edu
Date: 2022-11-06
Subject: groceries / family stuff / can we breathe this week?

Caro,

Studio just let out and my brain is oatmeal, but I wanted to sync on life logistics before we both disappear into Illustrator and Rhino.

1. Groceries: Can you grab rice, black beans, eggs, and coffee if you go to Aldi tomorrow? I’ll do a Costco run with Mateo next weekend when I’m in Aurora.

2. Money: I finally looked at my “family utilities support” tab again. Since August 2020 it’s basically the same pattern: $150–$200 a month from my fab lab paycheck going to Mom’s Nicor Gas and ComEd via Zelle. She told me last night they got another “reminder” letter, not as scary as the winter 2019–2020 shutoff notice but enough that my stomach did the thing.

3. Parents: Mom says Dad is still taking those occasional late-night courier runs in Chicago, the unlicensed ones, to chip away at her Rush Copley surgery bills. She called it “Miguel’s side job” again. I hate picturing him driving around at 2 a.m. for under-the-table pay, but I also get it.

4. Daniel: She also mentioned Daniel’s finally back on track after that suspension in May 2021, the hallway fight where the kid called their neighborhood “ghetto.” I still think about that assistant principal email when we talk about “equity in public space” in seminar.

5. Us: I have Dr. Chang this Thursday in DeGarmo (aka cry-about-studio o’clock), so I’ll be home after 5. Can we do a quiet dinner? No guests, no studio people, just us and maybe The Great British Bake Off in the background.

Text me if you think of anything else we need for the apartment. I’ll be home after I finish lasering these stupid façade panels.

Besos,
J

metadata.subject: groceries / family stuff / can we breathe this week?

artifacts.content:
From: jordan.e.ramirez@gmail.com
To: mariah.lopez@ilstu.edu
Date: 2023-01-24
Subject: leadership feelings dump before crit

Hey you,

Writing this instead of spiraling before tomorrow’s desk crit.

I’ve been thinking a lot about what kind of “leader” I actually want to be after graduation. Part of it is all the AIAS stuff, part of it is…everything else.

I keep going back to three things:

1) My dad picking up those sketchy overnight courier shifts in Chicago after Mom’s surgery at Rush Copley in 2019. Mom told me in that worried “Miguel’s side job” call in January 2021 that he was doing unlicensed late-night runs to pay down her medical debt. That picture of him driving around at 2 a.m. just to keep the house afloat is burned into my brain when people talk about “benefits” and “stability.”

2) Daniel’s suspension email from May 6, 2021 — “Incident involving Daniel Ramirez – May 6.” He gets racially harassed in a hallway, someone calls our block “ghetto,” and he’s the one who ends up suspended. Every time studio talks about “safe public space” I think about that hallway.

3) My own mess-ups. The academic integrity warning from Fall 2020 in Dr. Benson’s Building Systems class still sits in my file. And then last summer, June 14, 2022, when Eric at Lopez + Kwan messaged me in that “Pilsen retrofit hours” Teams chat and asked me to log 4–5 AXP hours for a Little Village community meeting I didn’t attend “so we can show more engagement.” I went along once and have regretted it since.

All of that is why I care so much about equity, and also why I’m low-key terrified of messing up again. Add on top of that the fact that most of my family still doesn’t know we’ve been together since October 2020, and I feel like I’m constantly editing myself depending on the room.

Anyway, thanks for being the one person I don’t have to translate for.

Love,
J

Hey Caro,

I hope you're doing well! I just wanted to catch up on some exciting news and my plans for the upcoming fall semester. As you know, I'm finishing up my final year in the B.Arch program at Illinois State University, and it's going to be a busy one with my role as the AIAS Chapter Vice President. Since I've been with AIAS for about three years now, moving into leadership roles since Fall 2019, I'm really looking forward to all the opportunities ahead.

I'm thrilled to tell you about a leadership conference happening this September that I'm planning to attend. It's right up my alley, focusing on leadership, professional practice, and advocacy. These are areas I've been exploring ever since I started with AIAS, but this conference promises a more structured approach. It's going to be perfect for me, especially since I'm interested in learning strategies for leading from the middle, which is crucial for my current vice president role. Plus, there will be discussions on equity, diversity, and inclusion in architectural leadership.

After graduation in May 2023, my goal is to start my AXP hours at a firm in Chicago that centers on community-focused projects, just like Lopez + Kwan where I'm interning. Growing up in Aurora, I saw firsthand how much of a difference thoughtful design can make, especially in neighborhoods with aging, inefficient buildings. I want to use architecture as a tool for equity, especially for working-class and immigrant communities. 

For the fall semester, my schedule is quite packed. I'm enrolled full-time, and it looks like it'll be intense but manageable. Here's a quick rundown:
- Mondays: Building Systems II and Comprehensive Design Studio, with AIAS meetings in the evening.
- Tuesdays: Architectural Theory and Criticism, and Urban Design Seminar, plus studio work.
- Wednesdays: More Building Systems II and studio time.
- Thursdays: Theory and Urban Design again, followed by AIAS general body meetings.
- Fridays: Open studio work in the morning, and then flexible afternoons for model building.

Luckily, I've managed to arrange my part-time job at the fabrication lab around all this, so I can attend the conference without any issues. And yes, I'm fully vaccinated and comfortable with attending in-person meetups, as long as health protocols are in place.

We'll need to catch up more once the semester starts! Maybe we can brainstorm some new ideas for our living room setup to better accommodate both our projects. Let me know what you think.

Take care,
Jordan

metadata.subject: Excited About the Upcoming Conference and Fall Plans
